title: Moser Flow: Divergence-based Generative Modeling on Manifolds [PDF]

## **Moser Flow: Divergence-based Generative Modeling on Manifolds**

**Noam Rozen**[1] **Aditya Grover**[2] _[,]_[3] **Maximilian Nickel**[2] **Yaron Lipman**[1] _[,]_[2] 1Weizmann Institute of Science 2Facebook AI Research 3UCLA

## **Abstract**

We are interested in learning generative models for complex geometries described via manifolds, such as spheres, tori, and other implicit surfaces. Current extensions of existing (Euclidean) generative models are restricted to specific geometries and typically suffer from high computational costs. We introduce _Moser Flow_ (MF), a new class of generative models within the family of continuous normalizing flows (CNF). MF also produces a CNF via a solution to the change-of-variable formula, however differently from other CNF methods, its model (learned) density is parameterized as the source (prior) density minus the _divergence_ of a neural network (NN). The divergence is a local, linear differential operator, easy to approximate and calculate on manifolds. Therefore, unlike other CNFs, MF does not require invoking or backpropagating through an ODE solver during training. Furthermore, representing the model density explicitly as the divergence of a NN rather than as a solution of an ODE facilitates learning high fidelity densities. Theoretically, we prove that MF constitutes a universal density approximator under suitable assumptions. Empirically, we demonstrate for the first time the use of flow models for sampling from general curved surfaces and achieve significant improvements in density estimation, sample quality, and training complexity over existing CNFs on challenging synthetic geometries and real-world benchmarks from the earth and climate sciences.

## **1 Introduction**

The major successes of deep generative models in recent years are primarily in domains involving Euclidean data, such as images (Dhariwal and Nichol, 2021), text (Brown et al., 2020), and video (Kumar et al., 2019). However, many kinds of scientific data in the real world lie in non-Euclidean spaces specified as manifolds. Examples include planetary-scale data for earth and climate sciences (Mathieu and Nickel, 2020), protein interactions and brain imaging data for life sciences (Gerber et al., 2010; Chen et al., 2012), as well as 3D shapes in computer graphics (Hoppe et al., 1992; Kazhdan et al., 2006). Existing (Euclidean) generative models cannot be effectively applied in these scenarios as they would tend to assign some probability mass to areas outside the natural geometry of these domains.

An effective way to impose geometric domain constraints for deep generative modeling is to design _normalizing flows_ that operate in the desired manifold space. A normalizing flow maps a prior (source) distribution to a target distribution via the change of variables formula (Rezende and Mohamed, 2015; Dinh et al., 2016; Papamakarios et al., 2019). Early work in this direction proposed invertible architectures for learning probability distributions directly over the specific manifolds defined over spheres and tori (Rezende et al., 2020). Recently, Mathieu and Nickel (2020) proposed to extend continuous normalizing flows (CNF) (Chen et al., 2018) for generative modeling over Riemannian manifolds wherein the flows are defined via vector fields on manifolds and computed as the solution to an associated ordinary differential equation (ODE). CNFs have the advantage that the neural

35th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2021).

network architectures parameterizing the flow need not be restricted via invertibility constraints. However, as we show in our experiments, existing CNFs such as FFJORD (Grathwohl et al., 2018) and Riemannian CNFs (Mathieu and Nickel, 2020) can be slow to converge and the generated samples can be inferior in capturing the details of high fidelity data densities. Moreover, it is a real challenge to apply Riemannian CNFs to complex geometries such as general curved surfaces.

To address these challenges, we propose Moser Flows (MF), a new class of deep generative models within the CNF family. An MF models the desired target density as the source density minus the divergence of an (unrestricted) neural network. The divergence is a local, linear differential operator, easy to approximate and calculate on manifolds. By drawing on classic results in differential geometry by Moser (1965) and Dacorogna and Moser (1990), we can show that this parameterization induces a CNF solution to the change-of-variables formula specified via an ODE. Since MFs directly parameterize the model density using the divergence, unlike other CNF methods, we do not require to explicitly solve the ODE for maximum likelihood training. At test-time, we use the ODE solver for generation. We derive extensions to MFs for Euclidean submanifolds that efficiently parameterize vector fields projected to the desired manifold domain. Theoretically, we prove that Moser Flows are universal generative models over Euclidean submanifolds. That is, given a Euclidean submanifold _M_ and a target continuous positive probability density _µ_ over _M_ , MFs can push arbitrary positive source density _ν_ over _M_ to densities _µ_ ¯ that are arbitrarily close to _µ_ .

We evaluate Moser Flows on a wide range of challenging real and synthetic problems defined over many different domains. On synthetic problems, we demonstrate improvements in convergence speed for attaining a desired level of details in generation quality. We then experiment with two kinds of complex geometries. First, we show significant improvements of 49% on average over Riemannian CNFs (Mathieu and Nickel, 2020) for density estimation as well as high-fidelity generation on 4 earth and climate science datasets corresponding to global locations of volcano eruptions, earthquakes, floods, and wildfires on spherical geometries. Next and last, we go beyond spherical geometries to demonstrate for the first time, generative models on general curved surfaces.

## **3 Moser Flow**

Moser (1965) and Dacorogna and Moser (1990) suggested a method for solving the normalizing equation, that is equation 2. Their method explicitly constructs a vector field _vt_ , and the flow it defines via equation 3 is guaranteed to solve equation 2. We start by introducing the method, adapted to our needs, followed by its application to generative modeling. We will use notations introduced above.

## **5 Experiments**

In all experiments, we modeled a manifold vector field as a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) _**u** θ ∈_ X( _M_ ), with parameters _θ_ . All models were trained using Adam optimizer (Kingma and Ba, 2014), and in all neural networks the activation is Softplus with _β_ = 100. Unless stated otherwise, we set _λ_ + = 0. We used an exact calculation of the divergence div _E_ ( _**u**_ ( _**x**_ )). We experimented with two kinds of manifolds.

**Flat Torus.** To test our method on Euclidean 2D data, we used _M_ as the flat torus, that is the unit square [ _−_ 1 _,_ 1][2] with opposite edges identified. This defines a manifold with no boundary which is locally isometric to the Euclidean plane. Due to this local isometry the Riemannian divergence on the flat

## **5.1 Toy distributions**

First, we consider a collection of challenging toy 2D datasets explored in prior works (Chen et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2021). We scale samples to lie in the flat torus [ _−_ 1 _,_ 1][2] and use _k_ = 1 for the positional encoding. Figure 2 depicts the input data samples, the generated samples _after_ training, and the learned distribution _µ_ ¯. In the top six datasets, the MLP architecture used for Moser Flows consists of 3 hidden layers with 256 units each, whereas in the bottom two we used 4 hidden layers with 256 neurons due to the higher complexity of these distributions. We set _λ−_ = 2.

## **5.3 Time evaluations**

To compare our method to Euclidean CNF methods, we compare Moser Flow with FFJORD (Grathwohl et al., 2018) on the flat torus. We consider a challenging density with high frequencies obtained via 800x600 images (Figure 4). We generate a new batch every iteration by sampling each pixel location with probability which is proportional to the pixel intensity. The architectures of both _**v** θ_ and the vector field defined in FFJORD are the same, namely an MLP with 4 hidden layers of 256 neurons each. To capture the higher frequencies in the image we use a positional encoding with _k_ = 8 for both methods. We used a batch size of 10k. We used learning rate of 1e-5 for Moser Flow and 1e-4 for FFJORD. We used _λ−_ = 2. Learning was stopped after 5k seconds. Figure 4 presents the results. Note that Moser Flow captures high-frequency details better than FFJORD. This is also expressed in the graph on the top right, showing how the NLL decreases faster for MF than FFJORD. ## **5.4 Earth and climate science data**

We evaluate our model on the earth and climate datasets gathered in Mathieu and Nickel (2020). The projection operator _π_ in this case is simply _π_ ( _**x**_ ) = _∥_ _**xx** ∥_[.][We parameterize] _**[ v]**[θ]_[as an MLP with] 6 hidden layers of 512 neurons each. ## **5.5 Curved surfaces**

We trained an SDF _f_ for the Stanford Bunny surface _M_ using the method in Gropp et al. (2020). To generate uniform ( _ν_ ) and data ( _µ_ ) samples over _M_ we first extract a mesh _M[′]_ from _f_ using the Marching Cubes algorithm (Lorensen and Cline, 1987) setting its resolution to 100x100x100. Then, to randomly choose a point uniformly from _M[′]_ we first randomly choose a face of the mesh with probability proportional to its area, and then randomly choose a point uniformly within that face. For target _µ_ we used clamped manifold harmonics to create a sequence of densities with increased complexity. To that end, we first computed the _k_ -th eigenfunction of the Laplace-Beltrami operator over _M[′]_ (we provide details on this computation in the supplementary), for the frequencies (eigenvalues) _k ∈{_ 10 _,_ 50 _,_ 500 _}_ . Next, we sampled the eigenfunctions at the faces’ centers, clamped their negative values, and normalized to get discrete probability densities over the faces of _M[′]_ . Then, to sample a point, we first choose a face at random based on this probability, and then random a point uniformly within that face. We take 500k i.i.d. samples of this distribution as our dataset. We take _**v** θ_ to be an MLP with 6 hidden layers of dimension 512. We use batch size of 10k for both the NLL loss and for the integral approximation; we ran for 1000 epochs with learning rate of 1e-4. We used _λ−_ = _λ_ + = 1. Figure 6 depict the results. Note that Moser Flow is able to learn the surface densities for all three frequencies.

## **6 Related Work**

In the following, we discuss related work on normalizing flows for manifold-valued data. On a high level, such methods can be divided into _Parametric_ versus _Riemannian_ methods. Parametric methods consist of a normalizing flow in the Euclidean space R _[n]_ , pushed-forward onto the manifold through an invertible map _ψ_ : R _[n] →M_ . However, to globally represent the manifold, _ψ_ needs to be a homeomorphism implying that _M_ and R _[n]_ are topologically equivalent, limiting the scope of that

approach. Existing methods in this class are often based on the exponential map exp _x_ : _TxM_ = _[∼]_ R _[n] →M_ of a manifold. This leads to the so called _wrapped_ distributions. This approach has been taken, for instance, by Falorsi et al. (2019) and Bose et al. (2020) to parametrize probability distributions on Lie groups and hyperbolic space. However, Parametric methods based on the exponential map often lead to numerical and computational challenges. For instance, in compact manifolds (e.g., spheres or the SO(3) group) computing the density of _wrapped_ distributions requires an infinite summation. On the hyperboloid, on the other hand, the exponential map is numerically not well-behaved far away from the origin (Dooley and Wildberger, 1993; Al-Mohy and Higham, 2010).

In contrast to Parametric methods, Riemannian methods operate directly on the manifold itself and, as such, avoid numerical instabilities that arise from the mapping onto the manifold. Early work in this class of models proposed transformations along geodesics on the hypersphere by evaluating the exponential map at the gradient of a scalar manifold function (Sei, 2011). Rezende et al. (2020) introduced _discrete Riemannian_ flows for hyperspheres and torii based on Möbius transformations and spherical splines. Mathieu and Nickel (2020) introduced _continuous_ flows on general Riemannian manifolds (RCNF). In contrast to discrete flows (e.g., Bose et al., 2020; Rezende et al., 2020), such time-continuous flows alleviate the previous topological constraints by parametrizing the flow as the solution to an ODE over the manifold (Grathwohl et al., 2018). Concurrently to RCNF, Lou et al. (2020) and Falorsi and Forré (2020) proposed related extensions of neural ODEs to smooth manifolds. Moser Flow also generates a CNF, however by limiting the flow space (albeit, not the generated distributions) it allows expressing the learned distribution as the divergence of a vector field.

## **7 Discussion and limitations**

We introduced Moser Flow, a generative model in the family of CNFs that represents the target density using the divergence operator applied to a vector valued neural network. The main benefits of MF stems from the simplicity and locality of the divergence operator. MFs circumvent the need to solve an ODE in the training process, and are thus applicable on a broad class of manifolds. Theoretically, we prove MF is a universal generative model, able to (approximately) generate arbitrary positive target densities from arbitrary positive prior densities. Empirically, we show MF enjoys favorable computational speed in comparison to previous CNF models, improves density estimation on spherical data compared to previous work by a large margin, and for the first time facilitate training a CNF over a general curved surface.

One important future work direction, and a current limitation, is scaling of MF to higher dimensions. This challenge can be roughly broken to three parts: First, the model probabilities _µ_ ¯ should be computed/approximated in log-scale, as probabilities are expected to decrease exponentially with the dimension. Second, the variance of the numerical approximations of the integral � _M[µ]_[¯] _[−][dV]_ will increase significantly in high dimensions and needs to be controlled. Third, the divergence term, div( _u_ ), is too costly to be computed exactly in high dimensions and needs to be approximated, similarly to other CNF approaches. Finally, our work suggests a novel generative model, and similarly to other generative models can be potentially used for generation of fake data and amplify harmful biases in the dataset. Mitigating such harms is an active and important area of ongoing research.

However, the most significant issue is the lack of methodological detail in the provided excerpt—particularly regarding how the divergence is computed on arbitrary manifolds, the architecture of the neural network, and the precise training objective—limiting immediate reproducibility. Additionally, while the theoretical claims are strong, the assumptions underlying the universality result (e.g., compactness, boundarylessness) are not thoroughly discussed in terms of practical implications. The experimental claims are promising but would be more convincing with additional baseline comparisons and ablation studies. Overall, the paper shows high potential in terms of originality, theoretical depth, and practical impact, but would benefit from greater exposition in the method section and more critical discussion of limitations.

comments.explanation:
Equation (12) defines the model density as $ ar{\mu} = 
u - \mathrm{div}(u)$. However, from the earlier PDE in equation (10), $ rac{d}{dt}\alpha_t = -\mathrm{div}(u)$, and with $ rac{d}{dt}\alpha_t = \mu - 
u$, we get $\mu - 
u = -\mathrm{div}(u)$, hence $\mu = 
u - \mathrm{div}(u)$. So in the steady-time case (constant vector field), $ ar{\mu}$ should equal $\mu$, consistent with setting $ ar{\mu} =

u = -\mathrm{div}(u)$. However, this relies on correctly interpreting the direction of time and flow. A reader might expect the density to evolve as $\partial_t \alpha_t = +\mathrm{div}(\cdot)$ depending on convention (e.g., Fokker-Planck vs. continuity equation). The authors follow the correct Moser convention, but the lack of explicit derivation from (10) to (12) risks confusion. While context resolves it (since (10) implies $\mu =

However, in the Euclidean submanifold section (equation 17), the loss is written using $\mathrm{div}_E(\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x}))$, and the density is $ ar{\mu} =

comments.title: Unclear justification for equivalence of losses in equations (14) and (16)
comments.quote:
an equivalent loss to the one presented in equation 14 is:

with _λ−_ + _λ_ + _≥_ 1. Empirically we found this loss favorable in some cases (i.e., with _λ_ + _>_ 0).
comments.explanation: The paper claims that equation (16), which includes both $\int \bar{\mu}_- dV$ and $\int \bar{\mu}_+ dV$, is 'equivalent' to equation (14), which only penalizes $\int \bar{\mu}_- dV$. The justification given is Lemma 1 and equation (13): $\int \bar{\mu}_+ dV = \int \bar{\mu}_- dV + 1$. While this identity holds, the losses are not functionally equivalent unless the hyperparameters are linked. For example, equation (14) uses $\lambda_- \int \bar{\mu}_- dV$, while equation (16) uses $\lambda_+ \int \bar{\mu}_+ dV + \lambda_- \int \bar{\mu}_- dV$. Substituting, the second loss becomes $(\lambda_+ + \lambda_-) \int \bar{\mu}_- dV + \lambda_+$, which differs from the first by a constant $\lambda_+$ and a rescaling of the penalty coefficient. Thus, the minimizers are the same only if $\lambda_+ = 0$, but the paper says $\lambda_+ > 0$ is used empirically. While both losses share the same minimum (as shown in Theorem 2) when $\lambda \geq 1$, their optimization dynamics differ. Calling them 'equivalent' is misleading — they are related, but not equivalent in the usual sense (same minimizers under same hyperparameters). A better term would be 'related' or 'alternate form'. This is a minor overstatement, but could cause confusion if a reader tries to reproduce results using 'equivalent' losses with the same $\lambda$ values.

comments.explanation: While the paper clearly explains how to define $\mathbf{u}$ on Euclidean submanifolds via projection (equation 18), it provides no details on how $u \in \mathfrak{X}(M)$ is parameterized for general Riemannian manifolds (e.g., sphere, hyperbolic space). The experiments include spherical geometries, so this is not just theoretical. The text merely says 'we used an MLP', but on a manifold that is not embedded in $\mathbb{R}^d$ with a projection map, how is the output of the MLP constrained to the tangent space? For example, on a sphere, does the network output $\mathbb{R}^3$ vectors projected to tangent space, or does it output coefficients in a local basis? This is crucial for reproducibility. The flat torus case is handled with periodic encoding, but that’s a special case. The general case is completely underspecified. While one might infer the method from the submanifold section, it’s not explicitly stated that the same projection trick is used for arbitrary embedded manifolds. This is a real gap in method description.

comments.title: Missing justification for universality in Theorem 3
comments.quote: we conclude this section by stating that the MF generative model over Euclidean submanifolds (defined with equations 12 and 18) is universal. That is, MFs can generate, arbitrarily well, any continuous target density _µ_ on a submanifold manifold _M ⊂_ R _[d]_ .
comments.explanation:
Theorem 3 asserts universality, but no proof sketch or reference to a constructive argument is provided in the main text (even though it's stated 'in the supplementary'). The claim relies on the ability of MLPs to approximate any tangent vector field and the solvability of $\mu = 
u - \mathrm{div}(u)$ for any $\mu, 
u$. But on a compact manifold, solving $\mathrm{div}(u) = 
u - \mu$ requires $\int (
u - \mu) dV = 0$, which holds, but existence of a smooth solution $u$ depends on elliptic PDE theory (e.g., Hodge decomposition). Moreover, MLPs must approximate such $u$ arbitrarily well — this requires more than universal approximation of functions; it requires approximation in a Sobolev-like norm involving derivatives. While plausible (and likely correct given the reference to Moser), the paper provides no justification in the main text. For a major theoretical claim like universality, a brief discussion of the PDE solvability and approximation capacity would help. As is, it feels asserted rather than proven, risking overclaim.

comments.title: Ambiguous interpretation of "49% improvement" in density estimation

comments.explanation: The term "49% improvement" is ambiguous: is it a 49% reduction in NLL, a 49% increase in log-likelihood, or improvement in some other metric? In generative modeling, improvements are typically reported in bits/dim or log-likelihood units, and percentage improvements can be misleading (e.g., going from -1.0 to -0.51 is a 49% improvement in the value, but not in any standard metric). The paper does not specify the metric or baseline. While such results may appear in the full paper’s experiments, in this excerpt, the claim lacks sufficient context to interpret. It could overstate the result — for example, if the baseline was poorly tuned. Given that the paper compares to a specific method (Riemannian CNF), the reader needs to know whether this is a relative improvement in NLL or some other performance proxy. This is a minor overclaim due to imprecise language, but common in machine learning papers. Still, for a careful reader, it raises questions about effect size.

comments.title: Potential sign error in Moser Flow density definition
comments.explanation: At first glance, the definition of the model density as _µ̄ = ν − div(u)_ raises concern because in many flow-based models, the change in density is tied to the divergence via addition (e.g., in continuous normalizing flows, d/dt log p_t = −div(v_t)). However, in the context of Moser’s method (1965) and Dacorogna–Moser theory, the construction relies on solving ∂ₜαₜ = −div(vₜ), where αₜ interpolates between ν and µ. With the linear interpolation αₜ = (1−t)ν + tµ, we get ∂ₜαₜ = µ − ν. Then, from the PDE ∂ₜαₜ + div(vₜ) = 0, it follows that div(vₜ) = ν − µ. In their construction, vₜ = (1−t)u (Equation 11), so integrating over t gives that the total effect relates to div(u). Plugging into the steady-state form (constant u), the resulting steady density satisfies ν − div(u) = µ, so µ̄ = ν − div(u) is indeed correct. Therefore, the sign is consistent with the underlying PDE framework. The authors motivate this via Equation 10 (implied from Equation 6 with constant u: div(u) = ν − µ), so the definition is correct. No error.

comments.title: Ambiguity in the interpolant time derivative
comments.quote: The time derivative of this interpolant, i.e., _dt[α][t]_[=] _[µ][ −][ν]_[,] does not depend on _t_ .
comments.explanation: The text states that d/dt αₜ = µ − ν, but αₜ = (1−t)ν + tµ, so indeed ∂ₜαₜ = µ − ν. However, in the context of the continuity equation ∂ₜαₜ + div(vₜ) = 0 (which is implied by the continuous normalization equation), one would expect ∂ₜαₜ = −div(vₜ). Thus, −div(vₜ) = µ − ν ⇒ div(vₜ) = ν − µ. But earlier, Equation 6 says div(uₜ) = ∂ₜαₜ. This appears contradictory—unless uₜ is not vₜ. From Equation 11 later, vₜ = (1−t)u, so div(vₜ) = (1−t)div(u). But from the PDE, if div(uₜ) = ∂ₜαₜ = µ − ν, then div(vₜ) = (1−t)(µ − ν), which is not constant. But we need div(vₜ) = ν − µ to satisfy the continuity equation. There's a sign and scaling mismatch. Resolving it: in Moser’s method, the correct PDE is ∂ₜαₜ + div(vₜ) = 0. If αₜ = (1−t)ν + tµ, then ∂ₜαₜ = µ − ν, so div(vₜ) = ν − µ. If we set vₜ = u for all t, then div(u) = ν − µ, so u is constant. But the paper sets uₜ = u (constant) and defines vₜ = (1−t)u (Equation 11), which gives div(vₜ) = (1−t)div(u). For this to equal ν − µ, we require (1−t)div(u) = ν − µ, which depends on t—contradicting the time-independence of the right-hand side. This is inconsistent. Alternatively, if the PDE is div(uₜ) = ∂ₜαₜ = µ − ν, and vₜ = (1−t)uₜ, then div(vₜ) = (1−t)(µ − ν), and ∂ₜαₜ + div(vₜ) = (µ − ν) + (1−t)(µ − ν) = (2−t)(µ − ν) ≠ 0. This does not satisfy the continuity equation. The only way this works is if vₜ is defined differently. Standard Moser flow uses vₜ = u (with div(u) = ν − µ) for all t, leading to the flow matching the linear interpolation. The choice vₜ = (1−t)u breaks this. Figure 1 suggests vt is time-varying, but the continuity equation must hold at each t. This suggests a fundamental error in the vector field definition. Unless Equation 8 (missing) defines vt differently, there's a real inconsistency. This undermines the theoretical foundation of Theorem 1. This is a serious issue.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of generalized KL divergence in loss analysis
comments.quote: The loss in equation 14 equals _D_ ( _µ,_ ¯ _µ_ +) _−_ E _µ_ log _µ_ + ( _λ −_ 1)  _M[µ]_[¯] _[−][dV]_[ .]
comments.explanation: At first glance, the notation _D_ ( _µ,_ ¯ _µ_ +) appears to denote the generalized KL divergence between the true target density _µ_ and the model’s truncated density _µ_ ¯+, which would make sense in context. However, the generalized KL divergence was previously defined as $\mathcal{D}_{\text{KL}}(f \| g) = \int f \log(f/g) dV - \int f dV + \int g dV$, with domain-specific arguments. Here, if _D_ ( _µ,_ ¯ _µ_ +) is meant to be $\mathcal{D}_{\text{KL}}(\mu \| \bar{\mu}_+) $, then the expression does not match the earlier definition: the required term $ - \int \mu dV + \int \bar{\mu}_+ dV $ is missing. Instead, the text uses Lemma 1 to replace integrals involving $\bar{\mu}$, but it skips the detailed expansion of $\mathcal{D}_{\text{KL}}(\mu \| \bar{\mu}_+)$. While the result (eventual equality to the loss) is likely correct, the derivation step is underspecified. A careful reader could reasonably wonder whether the sign or order of arguments was flipped, especially since the generalized KL is not symmetric. The intended identity appears to be asymptotic or approximate, but without showing the full expansion using the given definition, confusion arises.

comments.title: Inconsistency in treatment of integral of max function in loss equivalence
comments.quote: Lemma 1 and equation 13 imply that  _M[µ]_[¯][+] _[dV]_[=]  _M[µ]_[¯] _[−][dV]_[ +1][. Therefore,] an equivalent loss to the one presented in equation 14 is:
comments.explanation: The claim that $\int_M \bar{\mu}_+ dV = \int_M \bar{\mu}_- dV + 1$ is crucial for justifying the equivalence between loss variations. However, from equation 13, $\bar{\mu}_+ = \max(\bar{\mu}, \epsilon)$, and $\bar{\mu}_- = \min(\bar{\mu}, \epsilon)$, so pointwise $\bar{\mu} = \bar{\mu}_+ - \bar{\mu}_-$ only if $\bar{\mu} \leq \epsilon$ in regions where subtraction occurs—but this is not pointwise true. Rather, the identity $\bar{\mu} = \bar{\mu}_+ - \bar{\mu}_- $ does not hold; instead, $\bar{\mu}_+ = \bar{\mu}$ when $\bar{\mu} \geq \epsilon$, and $\epsilon$ otherwise, while $\bar{\mu}_- = \bar{\mu}$ when $\bar{\mu} < \epsilon$, $0$ otherwise. Therefore $\bar{\mu}_+ - \bar{\mu}_- \neq \bar{\mu}$ in general. The real reason $\int \bar{\mu}_+ dV = \int \bar{\mu}_- dV + 1$ must rely on Lemma 1 ($\int \bar{\mu} dV = 1$) and the identity $\bar{\mu}_+ + \bar{\mu}_- = \bar{\mu} + \epsilon$ only on the set $\{x : \bar{\mu}(x) < \epsilon\}$ is incorrect. A correct derivation would use $\bar{\mu}_+ = \bar{\mu} + \bar{\mu}_- - \epsilon$, which leads to $\int \bar{\mu}_+ dV = \int \bar{\mu} dV + \int \bar{\mu}_- dV - \epsilon \cdot \mathrm{vol}(M)$, not the claimed equality. Hence, unless $\epsilon$ and $\mathrm{vol}(M)$ are normalized (e.g., via rescaling $\nu$), this equality does not hold. The text says the source can be unnormalized later, but this is only mentioned after, creating a circular justification. So the logical step is questionable.

comments.title: Missing justification for uniqueness in Theorem 2
comments.quote: Uniqueness follows by considering an arbitrary minimizer _µ_ ¯ = _µ_ . Indeed, the minimal value of this loss is _−_ E _µ_ log _µ_ ¯. _µ_ Since such a minimizer and it is achieved by satisfies _D_ ( _µ,_ ¯ _µ_ +) = 0 and  _M[µ]_[¯] _[−][dV]_[= 0][, necessarily] _[µ]_[¯][ =] _[ µ]_[.]
comments.explanation: The proof sketch for Theorem 2 asserts uniqueness but does so poorly. It states that $D(\mu, \bar{\mu}_+) = 0$ implies $\bar{\mu} = \mu$, but $D$ is defined as a generalized KL divergence between $\mu$ and $\bar{\mu}_+$, and $D=0$ implies $\mu = \bar{\mu}_+$ almost everywhere. However, $\bar{\mu}_+ = \max(\bar{\mu}, \epsilon)$, so $\bar{\mu}_+ = \mu$ only if $\mu \geq \epsilon$, which is satisfied by assumption (“min_x \mu(x) > \epsilon”), so $\bar{\mu}_+ = \mu$ implies $\bar{\mu} = \mu$. That part can be justified. But the claim that $\int \bar{\mu}_- dV = 0$ implies $\bar{\mu} \geq 0$ is insufficient to force $\bar{\mu} = \mu$: it only forces $\bar{\mu} \geq \epsilon$, not equality to $\mu$. Uniqueness must come from the KL term, not the integral penalty. The text conflates two conditions: $D=0$ gives $\bar{\mu}_+ = \mu$, and if $\mu \geq \epsilon$, then $\bar{\mu}_+ = \mu$ implies $\bar{\mu} = \mu$. But the role of $\int \bar{\mu}_- dV = 0$ is only to prevent negative densities or values below $\epsilon$, not to enforce equality. The proof sketch misattributes the uniqueness to both terms, but only the KL term enforces identity. Thus, the reasoning is logically flawed, even if the conclusion is correct.

For a level set, the normal space is one-dimensional and spanned by $\nabla f(x)$, so _k_=1 and _N_ is the normalized gradient. But the paper does not state this. Later it says vector fields are defined via equation 18, which uses _π_(**x**), but for SDFs it only says 'we follow equation 18' without specifying how _π_ is computed. In practice, this is nontrivial: Newton iterations may be needed, and gradient clipping or normalization may affect training. The method claims exact divergence calculation (in experiments), but without specifying how the projection or normal is computed from the SDF, reproducibility suffers. For example, does the model use automatic differentiation to compute _∇f_, and then construct _P_, or does it rely on finite differences? The paper defers to supplementary materials, but in the main method description, key implementation choices are underspecified.

comments.title: Potential confusion in equation reference and scope of Lemma 1 application
comments.quote: Lemma 1 and equation 13 imply that  _M[µ]_[¯][+] _[dV]_[=]  _M[µ]_[¯] _[−][dV]_[ +1]
comments.explanation: Equation 13 defines $\bar{\mu}_+ = \max(\bar{\mu}, \epsilon)$, and $\bar{\mu}_-$ is not defined there but later used. The subsequent claim involving Lemma 1 ($\int \bar{\mu} dV = 1$) assumes $\bar{\mu}$ is defined everywhere, but $\bar{\mu}_-$ and $\bar{\mu}_+$ are auxiliary for the loss. The equality $\int \bar{\mu}_+ dV = \int \bar{\mu}_- dV + 1$ is not directly implied by Lemma 1 unless further decomposition is provided. comments.title: Inconsistent SDF for sphere in implicit surfaces section
comments.quote: the sphere, represented with the SDF _f_ ( _**x**_ ) = _∥_ _**x** ∥−_ 1
comments.explanation: At first glance, the signed distance function (SDF) for the unit sphere is correctly given as $ f(\mathbf{x}) = \|\mathbf{x}\| - 1 $. This is standard: the zero level set defines the unit sphere, and for $\|\mathbf{x}\| < 1$, $f < 0$, indicating inside points. However, in the notation and definitions provided in the FULL PAPER CONTEXT, no SDF is explicitly defined, so we rely on conventional definitions. The issue arises in Section 5.4, where the projection operator is described as $ \pi(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{\mathbf{x}}{\|\mathbf{x}\|} $. This normalization projects any nonzero point to the unit sphere, which aligns with the SDF $ f(\mathbf{x}) = \|\mathbf{x}\| - 1 $. Therefore, this is consistent. While some might expect $ f(\mathbf{x}) = 1 - \|\mathbf{x}\| $ to make inside positive, the sign convention in SDFs for level-set methods typically has negative inside, positive outside — which matches the current definition. Hence, after checking against standard conventions and downstream uses, no real inconsistency remains. This is correctly specified.

comments.explanation: The passage contains a clear OCR error: 'µ ¯' and 'µ > ¯ 0' appear where 'µ ¯ > 0' was intended. The sentence fragment 'If _µ >_ ¯ 0 over M then, together with the fact that _µ_ ¯ is realized by a CNF defined via _vt_ : _[ M]_[.]' is garbled but, from context, clearly intends to say that if $ \bar{\mu} > 0 $ on $ M $, then $ \bar{\mu} $ is a valid density realizable via the CNF defined by the vector field $ v_t $. Earlier, $ \bar{\mu}_+ = \max(\bar{\mu}, \epsilon) $ is defined, and in the proof sketch of Theorem 2, $ \bar{\mu}_- = \min(\bar{\mu}, \epsilon) $. In the loss, $ \int_M \bar{\mu}_- dV $ appears, but in the empirical formulation in Eq. (17), it becomes $ \lambda_- \frac{V(M)}{l} \sum \min(\bar{\mu}(y_j), \epsilon) $. All this is consistent. However, in Section 5.2 and Figure 3, the learned density is referred to as 'µ +', and the caption says 'the closer _µ_ ¯+ is to the generated density Φ _∗ν_'. This is potentially ambiguous: is $ \bar{\mu}_+ $ the model density or just a truncation used in regularization? The model density is $ \bar{\mu} $, and $ \bar{\mu}_+ $ is only used in the variation of the loss (Eq. 16). But in evaluation, they refer to $ \bar{\mu}_+ $ as the reported density estimate. This implies that the actual output density they use and visualize is $ \bar{\mu}_+ $, not $ \bar{\mu} $. This could mislead readers into thinking the model density is $ \bar{\mu}_+ $, whereas Eq. 12 defines it as $ \bar{\mu} = \nu - \text{div}(u) $. The truncation is only for regularization and numerical stability. The inconsistency between training definition and evaluation naming is subtle but real: they evaluate $ \bar{\mu}_+ $ while claiming the model learns $ \bar{\mu} $. This is a framing issue — not incorrect, but could cause confusion about what is actually being compared to $ \Phi_\ast \nu $.

comments.title: Unjustified claim that per-iteration time in MF is constant
comments.quote: Furthermore, as can be inspected in the per iteration time graph on the bottom-right, MF per iteration time does not increase during training, and is roughly 1-2 order of magnitudes faster than FFJORD iteration.
comments.explanation: The claim that 'MF per iteration time does not increase during training' is presented as a direct observation from Figure 4. However, in other CNFs like FFJORD, the computational cost of solving the ODE during training increases as the learned flow becomes more complex, requiring more solver steps. In MF, since no ODE is solved during training, the cost per iteration depends only on computing the divergence of the vector field, which for an MLP with fixed architecture and fixed batch size is constant. So, in principle, the training cost per iteration should indeed be stable. The authors get this right in theory. However, the graph in Figure 4 (referred to but not visible to us) is expected to show this behavior. The issue is not with the claim per se, but with the absence of any acknowledgment that this stability depends on fixed network size and batch size. The method could become slower if, for example, auto-differentiation cost scaled with network depth or if dynamic architectures were used. But under the described setup, it's valid. Hence, while the claim seems bold at first, it is well-justified by the method’s design. No real error remains.

The function $ \pi(x) $ is the closest point projection onto $ M $. comments.title: Inconsistency in use of $ \lambda $ between Theorem 2 and empirical loss variants
comments.quote: with _λ−_ + _λ_ + _≥_ 1. Empirically we found this loss favorable in some cases (i.e., with _λ_ + _>_ 0).
comments.explanation: In Theorem 2, the condition for uniqueness is $ \lambda \geq 1 $ in the original loss $ \mathcal{L} = -\log\text{like} + \lambda \int \bar{\mu}_- dV $. Then, the paper introduces an equivalent loss with $ \lambda_- + \lambda_+ \geq 1 $. The equivalence relies on $ \int \bar{\mu}_+ dV = \int \bar{\mu}_- dV + 1 $ (Lemma 1 and Eq. 13). Therefore, $ \lambda_- \int \bar{\mu}_- + \lambda_+ \int \bar{\mu}_+ = (\lambda_- + \lambda_+) \int \bar{\mu}_- + \lambda_+ $. Since constants don't affect optimization, the minimizer is the same as long as $ \lambda_- + \lambda_+ = \lambda \geq 1 $. So the condition $ \lambda_- + \lambda_+ \geq 1 $ preserves the theoretical guarantee. The authors correctly note this. However, in experiments, they use $ \lambda_- = 2, \lambda_+ = 0 $ or $ \lambda_- = \lambda_+ = 1 $. Both satisfy $ \lambda_- + \lambda_+ \geq 1 $. But in the loss with $ \lambda_+ > 0 $, the constant term $ \lambda_+ $ shifts the objective — which matters if one compares absolute loss values across runs. However, since only the model $ \bar{\mu} $ is used for sampling, this is fine. The issue is only in interpretation of the loss curve. The authors do not claim to compare absolute losses, so this is not a flaw. The theoretical foundation is intact. No real issue remains.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling and theoretically rigorous contribution to the field of generative modeling on non-Euclidean geometries. The motivation for "Moser Flow" (MF) is highly persuasive; by reformulating the density estimation problem through the divergence of a neural network rather than standard ODE-based approaches, the authors cleverly bypass the prohibitive computational costs of backpropagating through ODE solvers during training. The writing is technically precise, well-structured, and creates a clear logical progression from the limitations of existing Riemannian Continuous Normalizing Flows (CNFs) to the novelty of the proposed method. The most significant challenge for the authors moving forward will be ensuring clarity in the bridge between their "divergence-parameterized" training loss and the "ODE-solved" generation phase, as ensuring that these two stages remain consistent—and that the empirical performance gains hold up across the broader technical community—will be central to the paper’s impact. Overall, the work is mature, effectively motivated, and addresses an important gap in geometric deep learning.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

This paper introduces Moser Flow, a novel generative modeling framework within the continuous normalizing flow family that parameterizes target densities directly via the divergence of a neural network rather than through ODE-based transformations. The work is ambitious and addresses an important open problem: generative modeling on general Riemannian manifolds. The clarity of exposition is generally strong, particularly in the abstract and introduction, which effectively motivate the limitations of existing CNF approaches and articulate the key technical innovation. However, several significant issues require attention. First, the paper's central theoretical claim—that Moser Flow constitutes a universal density approximator—is stated but not substantiated with sufficient rigor in the provided excerpt; the proof sketch and the precise assumptions under which universality holds need to be presented more concretely. Second, while the divergence-based parameterization is correctly identified as computationally advantageous, the paper does not adequately address the practical challenges of computing divergences on arbitrary manifolds, including how to handle the metric-dependent terms and boundary conditions that arise on compact manifolds without boundary. Third, the empirical evaluation, though promising, compares primarily against Riemannian CNFs; a more thorough ablation study is needed to isolate whether the improvements stem from the divergence-based parameterization itself, from architectural differences, or from optimization advantages. Finally, the paper would benefit from a more explicit discussion of test-time generation costs, as the reliance on an ODE solver for sampling could undermine the claimed efficiency advantages in certain applications.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This paper introduces Moser Flow, a divergence-based parameterization for continuous normalizing flows on Riemannian manifolds that aims to bypass costly ODE solvers during training while targeting complex, non-Euclidean scientific data. The motivation is well-grounded, and the proposed approach offers a theoretically appealing alternative to existing Riemannian CNFs by directly modeling the target density as the prior minus a neural divergence term, with encouraging preliminary empirical results on spherical benchmarks. However, several critical issues must be resolved to establish the paper’s rigor and clarity. Most notably, the subtractive divergence formulation lacks an explicit mechanism to guarantee the non-negativity of the learned density, which is fundamental to probabilistic validity and requires either a rigorous theoretical bound under the stated assumptions or a practical constraint with clear justification. Additionally, while the authors claim to avoid ODE backpropagation during training, the exact gradient computation pipeline with respect to the network parameters must be mathematically explicit to ensure reproducibility and to clarify how density consistency and invertibility are preserved without explicit time integration. Finally, the manuscript’s clarity is currently hampered by pervasive PDF extraction artifacts, truncated sections (e.g., the Preliminaries cut off mid-sentence), and inconsistent typography, and the novelty claim regarding sampling on “general curved surfaces” should be carefully benchmarked against recent diffusion and flow methods on meshes to accurately position the contribution. With tighter theoretical justification for density positivity and gradient computation, thorough proofreading, and a more precise literature comparison, this work has strong potential but requires substantial technical and editorial refinement before acceptance.

comments.title: Incorrect claim of normal-direction constancy for parameterized vector field
comments.quote: The vector field _**u**_ in equation 18 is constant along normal directions to the manifold at _**x**_ (since _π_ ( _**x**_ ) is constant in normal directions). If _**n** ∈ N_ _**x** M_ , then in particular
comments.explanation: The authors claim that the vector field \mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{P}_{\pi(\mathbf{x})} \mathbf{v}_\theta(\mathbf{x}) is constant along normal directions solely because the closest-point projection \pi(\mathbf{x}) is constant in those directions. However, \mathbf{v}_\theta is defined as an MLP mapping \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathbb{R}^d, which inherently varies with \mathbf{x} in all directions, including normal directions. Consequently, \mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x}) is not generally constant along normals; its normal derivative contains the term \mathbf{P}_{\pi(\mathbf{x})} \nabla_{\mathbf{n}} \mathbf{v}_\theta(\mathbf{x}), which is non-zero unless \mathbf{v}_\theta is explicitly constructed to be normal-direction invariant. This constancy condition is explicitly stated as a prerequisite for Lemma 2 to hold exactly (\text{div}_M \mathbf{u} = \text{div}_E \mathbf{u}). Without it, the Riemannian divergence on \mathcal{M} differs from the Euclidean divergence by additional curvature and normal-derivative terms. The claim appears to be a logical oversight or an unacknowledged approximation that impacts the exactness of the divergence computation on curved manifolds.

comments.title: Identity of the Normalization Penalty Term
comments.quote: The loss in equation 14 equals _D_ ( _µ,_ ¯ _µ_ +) _−_ E _µ_ log _µ_ + ( _λ −_ 1)  _M[µ]_[¯] _[−][dV]_[ .
comments.explanation: The authors claim that tuning the hyperparameter λ controls the integral term ∫_M (μ̄ − 1) dV. However, by Lemma 1 and Stokes' Theorem, ∫_M μ̄ dV = 1 holds identically for any divergence-based density parameterization on a boundaryless manifold. Thus, the continuous integral term is exactly zero and cannot influence the optimization or uniqueness proof. The authors likely intended to penalize ∫_M (μ̄_+ − 1) dV (since the truncation μ̄_+ = max(μ̄, ε) shifts probability mass away from 1) or are relying on the discrete Monte Carlo approximation where the sum is not exactly zero. As written, the mathematical justification for including this term and interpreting λ's effect on it is flawed.

comments.title: Sign Convention and Labeling of Metrics in Table 1
comments.explanation: Table 1 reports 'Negative log-likelihood scores' but contains negative values, with the most negative values (e.g., -2.02 for Volcano) bolded as best results. Standard NLL is defined as −E[log p(x)] and is inherently non-negative. The reported values are consistent with log-likelihoods E[log p(x)] (which are ≤ 0), but lower (more negative) log-likelihoods indicate worse performance, contradicting the bolding convention that implies lower is better. Alternatively, if these are indeed NLL values, the label or sign convention is incorrect. This ambiguity prevents readers from correctly interpreting the magnitude and direction of MF's claimed performance gains.

comments.title: Ambiguity regarding log-scale computation of unclamped density
comments.quote: First, the model probabilities $\bar{\mu}$ should be computed/approximated in log-scale, as probabilities are expected to decrease exponentially with the dimension.
comments.explanation: It is standard in high-dimensional generative modeling to compute negative log-likelihoods to avoid numerical underflow, and the authors correctly note this necessity. However, in Moser Flow, the base learned density is intrinsically parameterized as $\bar{\mu} = \nu - \text{div}(u_\theta)$. Without the subsequent clamping operation, this expression is not guaranteed to be strictly positive; the divergence term can yield substantial negative values, particularly in high dimensions or in regions where the neural vector field's trace is large. Taking $\log \bar{\mu}$ directly would be mathematically undefined. The authors likely implicitly assume the log-scale discussion applies to $\log \bar{\mu}_+$ or the log-likelihood after enforcing the $\epsilon$-positivity constraint. Explicitly stating that the log computation applies to the *clamped* density would prevent ambiguity when scaling the method to high-dimensional domains.

Clarifying that parametric methods usually rely on *local* diffeomorphisms or multi-chart covers would sharpen the contrast with MF's intrinsic ODE formulation. comments.title: Symbol mismatch in pullback/pushforward notation
comments.quote:
By pulling-back _µ_ ˆ according to _Φ_ and asking it to equal to the prior density _ν_, we get the manifold version of the standard normalizing equation:
_ν̂ = Φ_∗ µ_ˆ_ (2)
comments.explanation: In differential geometry, the standard notation is _Φ_*_ for pushforward of forms or measures and _Φ^*_ for pullback. The text explicitly uses the phrase "pull-back" but writes _Φ_*_. Given the definition that _Φ(z) = x_ (mapping the prior _z_ to the target _x_), the correct change-of-variable formula is typically the pushforward of the prior: _Φ_* ν̂ = µ̂_, or equivalently, the pullback of the target: _ν̂ = Φ^* µ̂_. Using the symbol _Φ_*_ to denote the pullback of _µ̂_ contradicts standard convention and the text's own definition of _Φ_.

comments.title: Inconsistent description of loss terms
comments.quote: The first term in the loss is approximated by the empirical mean computed with the observations _X_, i.e., This term is merely the negative log likelihood of the observations. The second term in the loss is approximated by the empirical mean... This term is merely the negative log likelihood...
comments.explanation: The text assigns the property "This term is merely the negative log likelihood of the observations" to both "The first term" and "The second term" in the loss function. However, Equation (14) defines the loss as a sum of the negative log likelihood (first term) and a divergence penalty term (second term). The prose repeats the same description for both terms, which is confusing and likely an artifact of the OCR or text editing process rather than a mathematical error, but it renders the text logically inconsistent.

comments.title: Broken notation in constraint specification
comments.quote: while making sure of i.i.d. observations _µ ¯ ≥ X ε_
comments.explanation: The notation _µ ¯ ≥ X ε_ is problematic. _µ_ and _µ_ ¯ are probability density functions (or their volume forms), while _X_ represents the dataset (set of observations). The constraint _µ_ ¯ ≥ _ε_ should apply to the model density value at a point (or the volume form), not involve the dataset _X_. The symbol _X_ appears to be a remnant of the sentence structure ("observations from X such that..."), but the integration of the dataset variable into the inequality is nonsensical. It should read _µ_ ¯(x) ≥ _ε_ or simply _µ_ ¯ ≥ _ε_.

comments.title: Underspecified method for solving ODE on tori
comments.quote: To solve an ODE on the torus we simply solve it for the periodic function and wrap the result back to [_−_ 1 _,_ 1][2].
comments.explanation: This sentence describes the numerical implementation for solving the flow on a torus without providing sufficient technical detail for reproducibility. Simply solving the ODE on a periodic domain and "wrapping" back requires handling the periodic boundary conditions and the phase integration carefully (e.g., avoiding jumps in the coordinates or solutions). The implication that it is as simple as a coordinate wrap could be misleading to readers unfamiliar with integrating periodic vector fields, as numerical stability depends on the specific method used (e.g., adapting the step size or using a phase-amplitude formulation).

comments.title: Typo in projection operator definition
comments.quote: The projection operator _π_ in this case is simply _π_ ( _**x**_ ) = _∥_ _**xx** ∥
comments.explanation: The notation $\|\mathbf{xx}\|$ is nonsensical in this context. Context indicates $\pi$ is the closest point projection. For a sphere (implied by the location of the experiment and the mention of 'projection operator'), the intended formula is likely $\pi(\mathbf{x}) = \|\mathbf{x}\|$ (or $\|\mathbf{x}\| - 1$ if radius-normalized). 'xx' appears to be an OCR artifact (double character or formatting error) rather than a meaningful mathematical term.

comments.title: Logical gap in explaining density convergence
comments.quote: This is due to the fact that a higher value of $\lambda$ leads to a lower value of $\int_M \bar{\mu}^-$.
comments.explanation: The text attributes the convergence of $\mu_+$ (the positive part of the model density) to the push-forward density $\Phi_\ast \nu$ to the reduction in volume deficit $\int \bar{\mu}^-$. However, minimizing the volume deficit only guarantees that the density integrates to 1. It does not strictly guarantee pointwise non-negativity ($\bar{\mu} \ge 0$), which is necessary for $\mu_+$ to match $\Phi_\ast \nu$ everywhere. While the $-\log \bar{\mu}$ term in the loss function penalizes negativity, equating pointwise convergence solely to the integral constraint is a logical oversimplification.

While the $-\log \bar{\mu}$ term in the loss function penalizes negativity, equating pointwise convergence solely to the integral constraint is a logical oversimplification.
